data.title: Alphabet: Waymo's Importance Is Growing
data.description:
While Google's (NASDAQ:GOOG)(NASDAQ:GOOGL)(TSX:GOOG:CA) core search business faces mounting competitive pressure from AI-enabled alternatives, the company is also realizing the benefits of AI in other areas, including self-driving vehicles. Waymo is the clear leader in the robotaxi market but hasn't captured the hearts of investors, due in large part to concerns over scalability.

As I previously suggested, the robotaxi market probably isn’t large enough to move the needle for Google. There are adjacent opportunities is areas like logistics, though, in addition to a large licensing opportunity. Based on market capitalization, investors appear to be expecting Tesla (TSLA) to be the dominant force across most self-driving markets. Tesla’s advantage isn’t as large as imagined, though, due to the rapid scaling of Waymo's data acquisition capabilities. If Waymo is able to introduce a generalized self-driving solution that utilizes a low-cost sensor package in the next few years, it would likely create enough value to be meaningful for Alphabet.

While Waymo almost certainly has a vision-only, end-to-end model that performs at a high level, the performance of this approach must be considered insufficient for deployment in the near-term. The company's current system requires LiDAR, radar, detailed maps and still appears to leverage some heuristics. The system's performance under real-world conditions has been extremely strong so far, though.

Data from tens of millions of miles of real-world driving illustrates that Waymo's vehicles are safer than the average human driver, even if they must regularly request guidance from remote operators. Performance data isn't available for Waymo, but Cruise's remote assistants reportedly had to intervene around every 5 miles. Uber (UBER) has also stated that opt-in rates and user feedback for the service are strong.

It is worth noting that Waymo's data doesn't take into account who is at fault, meaning the incident rate of its vehicles where it is at fault is likely significantly lower than what has been reported. Waymo's performance is also fairly consistent across cities, unlike humans.

Data also suggests that Waymo's vehicles are particularly adept at reducing risk for vulnerable road users, and most collisions that Waymo vehicles have been involved in appear to be very minor. Waymo's vehicles are also good at avoiding crashes at intersections, which is likely due to an ability to identify vehicles running red lights.

Tesla's planned robotaxi launch in Austin suggests that it is now comfortable with the performance of its solution, even if there is currently limited publicly available data demonstrating this. Assuming that this is the case, Tesla's lead in developing a vision-only, end-to-end system has likely been driven by its ability to collect more data than competitors. Tesla’s data moat may not be as big as people think, though, particularly if it has already collected enough data to train a viable model. It is unknown how much real-world data Tesla has access to in total, but a cumulative total of around 3.5 billion miles have been driven with FSD. There is also the issue of data quality to consider. Tesla's data is far more diverse than Waymo's, but a considerable portion of it is also likely to be low-value highway miles.

Through the end of 2024, Waymo had driven roughly 50 million rider-only miles. The company is now performing roughly 250,000 paid rides per week across San Francisco, Phoenix, Los Angeles and Austin with a commercial fleet containing over 1,500 vehicles. Uber recently stated that Waymo had around 100 cars operating in Austin and that these vehicles are performing more trips per day than 99% of Uber’s drivers in Austin. Waymo most recently expanded to Silicon Valley and Austin and plans on launching services in Atlanta, Washington DC and Miami in the near future. The company also recently began mapping streets in Tokyo with 25 human-driven vehicles, the company's first foray internationally.

While growth is moderating, Waymo will continue to rapidly ramp its services over the next few years, which is demonstrated by its manufacturing plans. Waymo plans on producing more than 2,000 robotaxis over the next year at its Phoenix manufacturing facility, which is operated in partnership with Magna. The plant equips vehicles (Jaguar I-PACEs) with Waymo's technology and fully prepares them for robotaxi operations. The facility also has the flexibility to equip other vehicles with Waymo's technology, including the Zeekr RT. Waymo plans on introducing automation over time, enabling the facility to expand its capacity into the tens of thousands of vehicles annually. As a result, Waymo can probably reach 3 billion cumulative miles before 2030, and hence, it is reasonable to expect that the company will have a functional vision-only, end-to-end model by then.

This would allow Waymo's robotaxi service to scale faster and would significantly reduce capital requirements. It would also open up the licensing opportunity, an area that Waymo already appears to be shifting its focus towards. Waymo and Toyota are collaborating on ADAS and self-driving technology for personal cars. The scope of the collaboration isn't really clear at this stage, but it appears that Waymo is expanding its horizons beyond robotaxis. Alphabet's CEO has also suggested personal ownership of Waymo vehicles is a possibility.

Alphabet's share price has come under pressure in recent months due to growing concerns about the impact of LLMs on its search business. While search remains critical to Google, YouTube, subscriptions and its cloud business have become value drivers. Competitive pressures are also increasing the importance of Alphabet's other businesses.

Waymo was most recently valued at more than 45 billion USD, a drop in the ocean compared to Alphabet's ~1.8 trillion USD market capitalization. Waymo could have enormous potential if Alphabet is sufficiently ambitious, though, as evidenced by Tesla's market capitalization. Tesla is much less unique than many believe, and Waymo is in a strong position to maintain its robotaxi lead, as well as extend this advantage to adjacent markets.

data.title: May FOMC Meeting: Waiting For More Clarity
data.description:
By Seema Shah, Chief Global Strategist and Christian Floro, CFA, CMT, Market Strategist

As expected, the Federal Open Market Committee decided to keep its benchmark policy rate unchanged at 4.25%–4.50% at its May meeting.

The Fed continues to see the U.S. economy as resilient and is waiting to see if the weakness in soft data translates into weakness in hard data. With risks to their dual mandate rising, the Fed prefers to wait for additional clarity before adjusting policy, especially as the outlook remains extremely uncertain.

The current assessment

While survey data continued to deteriorate as sentiment data has soured, suggesting businesses and households have postponed decisions amid trade policy uncertainty, the negative impacts of the tariff shock have not shown up in actual economic data.

The Fed continues to see solid current labor market conditions, inflation moving sideways at a fairly low level, and longer-term inflation expectations well-anchored.

They also continue to characterize broad economic activity as solid, downplaying the weak Q1 GDP print as a function of swings in net exports. This likely stemmed from front-loaded imports ahead of tariff deadlines, which provides little useful information.

The Fed did caution that risks of a stagflationary environment - higher unemployment and higher inflation - have risen, though Fed Chair Jerome Powell emphasized the need for more data to determine the appropriate stance of monetary policy.

Uncertainty is extremely elevated, as it’s yet to be seen whether the current level of tariffs will be sustained, and to what extent they may evolve in the coming months as potential trade deals emerge.

Powell remains confident that the Fed is well positioned to wait for additional clarity on how trade policy will impact the economy, as the costs of postponing monetary action are low. As a result, there is no immediate urgency to reduce policy rates.

Asked about political jawboning from President Donald Trump, Powell stressed that it does not affect the Fed’s job at all. Moreover, he also dismissed questions on whether he would step down from the Fed after his position as Fed Chair ends in May 2026.

Policy outlook

While the brunt of the tariff shock has yet to meaningfully impact hard economic data just yet, it may be only a matter of time, especially if existing tariffs are sustained at their currently elevated levels. With both inflation and unemployment potentially increasing as a result, the Fed has been plunged into an almost impossible situation.

The Fed is also beholden to fiscal policy - itself incredibly uncertain - and this too will dictate both the timing and magnitude of their future monetary policy decisions.

Complicating matters more, recent headlines suggesting President Trump is taking a hardline approach to China trade negotiations further reinforces the uncomfortable position for the Fed.

In this situation, what else can the Fed do but sit on its hands? Rate cuts will be required but, increasingly, it seems that they will need to wait until late Q3 before the window of opportunity opens.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Please don't leave political comments on other articles or posts on the site.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

More on Today's Markets:

Moderation Guidelines:

We remove comments under the following categories:

  • Personal attacks on another user account
  • Anti-Vaxxer or covid related misinformation

  • Inciting violence messages, encouraging hate groups and political violence.

Regardless of which side of the political divide you find yourself, please be courteous and don't direct abuse at other users.

For any issue with regards to comments please email us at : moderation@seekingalpha.com.

data.title: Bitcoin Busts Through $100,000, Eyeing New Targets On BITB (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
After three long months, Bitcoin finally recaptured the $100,000 level on news that the US and UK reached a trade deal. It was a general risk-on sentiment that helped lift the world’s biggest cryptocurrency to multi-month highs, a more than 30% rally from its April lows.

What I have found particularly interesting is that bitcoin has traded, at times, like a defensive asset. It and gold have traded places as the major macro asset to own amid uncertainty about where the global economy is headed. For now, though, bitcoin may be turning back to its risk-on status as volatility has subsided in equities.

I was cautious about the Bitwise Bitcoin ETF (NYSEARCA:BITB) last July. Technicals were concerning, in my view, back then, and the fund went on to tag key support and a long-term buy point that I had outlined on the chart. With shares up 78% since then (and much of that gain coming just in the last handful of weeks), I am upgrading BITB to a buy based on sanguine momentum factors and a macro backdrop that could support bitcoin writ large.

Bitcoin Rallies Through $100,000, a Three-Month High

According to the issuer, BITB allows investors to gain convenient, low-cost exposure to bitcoin through a professionally managed ETF. This may avoid the cost, complexity, and custody concerns of owning Bitcoin directly. Bitcoin has distinct return patterns and historically low correlations to traditional stocks and bonds. As a result, Bitcoin can offer long-term diversification and risk management benefits to an overall investment portfolio.

BIT is a medium-sized ETF, now with $3.7 billion in assets under management as of May 7, 2025. That’s up significantly from just $2.3 billion at the start of the second half of last year. Indeed, money has poured into crypto lately. BofA points out that as of late April, flows have climbed markedly.

Crypto Inflows Coming Back

BITB features a low annual expense ratio of 20 basis points and, of course, does not pay a dividend. Its share-price momentum is very strong right now, and I think that will persist. I’ll outline later that if we see the all-time high eclipsed on both BITB and bitcoin, then a major leg higher could ensue. BITB is volatile, as prospective investors probably would surmise, as a result of the large daily swings in what is generally among the more stable tokens (sans stablecoins).

BITB scores well with its ETF liquidity grade, according to Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system—average daily volume is above 1.3 million shares, while the median 30-day bid/ask spread is tight at just four basis points, per Bitwise.

Mentioned previously, bitcoin and gold are doing a bit of a dance right now. Bitcoin has clearly gained the lead step as it notches multi-month highs and gold settles in below $3,500 per ounce. So, I expect BITB’s Sharpe Ratio to improve as the second quarter progresses.

Gold Had A Huge Rally, But Bitcoin With Emerging Relative Strength Lately

Seasonally, there’s reason to be bullish over the months ahead, too. According to price data going back to 2010, the May through July stretch has historically been very strong. While calendar trends should not be taken in isolation, it’s a sanguine factor to weigh.

Bitcoin: Bullish Seasonal Trends Through July

The Technical Take

Since Bitcoin does not produce cash flow and there are no financial statements to parse through, the chart should be analyzed closely. Notice in the graph below that Bitcoin itself held key long-term support with precision in early April. The $75,000 level just about matched the March 2024 peak—a time that marked the start of a long consolidation. After an upside breakout following President Trump’s win last November, a fast rally to near $110,000 ensued.

But take a look at where support initially emerged—in the low $90k zone. Once it was breached, the bears took control, bringing bitcoin to the early 2024 mark. The token also dropped below its long-term 200-day moving average and the 50dma. The key, however, was that the 200dma remained trending higher, suggesting that the long-term trend was not damaged.

Today, with a new high in the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the chart, we find that the price thrust has confirmed with momentum, which I like to see. It’s also consistent that a bullish RSI divergence occurred at the April nadir, helping to cement a bottom. A key risk is that there remains a high amount of bitcoin traded between $100,000 and $110,000—I boxed the volume increase around the turn of the year, and that could be bearish supply. Thus, the bulls still have some work cut out for them.

Bitcoin: $110,000 All-Time High In View, $74,000 Long-Term Support, Bullish RSI Confirmation, Rising 200dma

Looking closer in on BITB, a downtrend resistance line was broken in April. Then, a bull flag pattern emerged—the subsequent breakout just this week creates a measured move upside price objective to about $63 based on the height of the pole and depth of the flag. Near-term support is between $50 and $51.

BITB: Targeting $63 As Momentum Builds, $50 Support

The Bottom Line

I have a buy rating on BITB. I see the spot bitcoin ETF as featuring strong technicals, bullish seasonality, and high relative strength to the stock market and gold lately.

data.title: Alsea: Wraps Chipotle Into Its Portfolio
data.description:
Alsea (OTCPK:ALSSF) just had the kind of quarter that makes you raise one eyebrow and scratch the other. Revenues crept up about 10%, but EPS took a 44% nosedive like it slipped on a wet floor.

Still, heads definitely turned when word got out that Chipotle Mexican Grill (CMG) is joining Alsea’s lineup in Mexico, with openings set to start sizzling in Q1 FY 2026. This should be the eighth country where the fast-casual fad will open its doors, joining the United States (of course), Canada, the United Kingdom, Germany, Kuwait and the UAE.

It’s important to remember that aside from Alshaya Group—which operates Chipotle’s Middle Eastern locations—and now Alsea, all of the locations in North America and Europe are company-owned.

That’s one of the things that makes this special.

But will Chipotle’s move into Mexico go down easy or end up more like their awkward UK debut?

I mean, yeah, Mexican food in Mexico (shocking, I know), but just being Mexican-inspired might not cut it. And let’s not forget the local heavy-hitters already holding their ground.

The key will be marketing strategies to cater to local preferences.

Alsea will play a central role in how that translates—both in terms of price (which will likely be adjusted to suit the middle-income Mexican consumer) and menu (to position itself as a differentiator from local chains).

Overall, Alsea continues to see headwinds in Europe despite a recovery in Mexico and some Latin American countries. They reported revenue of MXN 20.03 billion – growing in double digits, as I said. However, EBITDA declined approximately 9.1% year-over-year (11.7% margin, below historical average) due to a few factors:

  1. The depreciation of the Mexican peso continues to be a headwind for operations in Mexico. This is the fastest-growing country, but when we convert Alsea’s results to euros, for example, we see that revenue did not grow by double digits, but fell by 11.14% in the same period;
  2. Three weeks of work stoppages in Chile;
  3. Macroeconomic pressures in most of the markets in which Alsea operates, especially in Europe, which still has historically high-interest rates;
  4. The company remained focused on traffic. This meant they didn’t pass on increases in cost centers to guests. On the bottom line, restaurant-level margins declined.

Since Mexico represented approximately 67% of total EBITDA (EU around 22% and LATAM at 11%), both Euro-denominated revenue and USD-based input costs cause margin compression in the segment. This has been the core of my thesis so far, and it looks like these headwinds continue to blow.

Let's take a brand by brand look here:

  • Starbucks (SBUX) Alsea reported same-store sales growth of 4.7% year-over-year. In Mexico, same-store sales increased 3.5%, extending a streak of positive same-store sales. In Europe, same-store sales fell 2.2%, recovering post-boycott traffic and still suffering from a value-driven environment. In South America, same-store sales grew 18.6%, but when excluding Argentina, they fell 6.7%;
  • For Domino's Pizza (DPZ) Alsea, same-store sales grew 2.6% year-over-year, slowing slightly sequentially. In Mexico, same-store sales grew 1.5%, also down from the mid-single-digit year-over-year growth of the previous quarter. In Spain, Alsea grew 2.4% thanks to menu innovations such as the Croissantizzima. In Colombia, growth was very strong, reaching 10.57%;
  • At Burger King (QSR) Alsea, same-store sales fell 4.8% year-over-year in Argentina and also fell about 7.8% in Mexico;
  • For Alsea’s full-service brands, the company saw same-store sales grow 3.4% year over year, slowing slightly sequentially but holding steady. Vips Mexico grew 2.8%. Italianni’s and Chili’s (EAT) reported positive single-digit same-store sales. In Spain, Vips was up 3.5% and Gino’s was up 2.2%.

It looks like a patchwork, I know. But just take a look at it on a graph, and you'll see what I mean:

On the bottom line, Alsea reported a net profit of MXN 227.4 million—a 44% drop year-over-year. This brought the reported diluted EPS to MXN 0.28, down from MXN 0.50 in the same period last year.

Due to operating asset headwinds, OCF was negative MXN 1.16 billion. With Capex of MXN 1.12 billion, Alsea reported negative FCF of MXN 2.28 billion. The solution? Increase debt. They issued about MXN 953 million, spent about MXN 230.4 million on buybacks and have a cash deficit of over MXN 2 billion.

That’s pushed long-term debt to nearly MXN 30 billion — the highest it’s been in at least a decade. That’s a debt/EBITDA ratio of just over 3x. High, but not too worrying yet.

How About Now — Is It Finally a Buy?

Fundamentally, things haven't improved yet. The Mexican peso continues to weaken against the Euro, and Starbucks' weakness in Europe is not doing enough to offset this.

But is this a buying window given Domino's growth and the resilience of the FSRs?

I have a PT of around $2. Anyway, let's take another look at these multiples, focusing mainly on EV to calculate the weight of the post-debt capital structure.

Analyst consensus tells us that FY 2025 revenue will be around $4.47 billion. With 803.8 million shares outstanding, RPS would be around $5.56. With an EV of $4.31 billion, Alsea shares are trading at an EV/S of 0.96x.

Peer comparisons include Arcos Dorados (ARCO) with an EV/S multiple of 0.69x. In the FSR (restaurant chains with majority corporate ownership), we have Bloomin' Brands (BLMN) with a multiple of 0.74x. The restaurant industry median is 1.12x.

All of this tells us that no, Alsea does not look like a bargain. It's a company that is trading at an EV/S below the industry median, but still above other companies that are being affected by the decline in consumer discretionary.

Another interesting perspective to consider is that Alsea is trading at an EV/S below the industry median because it is facing headwinds that are compressing its margin. As I said before, the company reported an EBITDA margin of approximately 9.9% in the TTM, about 630 bps lower than its 5-year average.

I want to project EBITDA based on the analyst consensus revenue of $4.47 billion. Let's assume an EBITDA margin for FY 2025 between 10% and 11% based on a projection that considers a weaker dollar against the Mexican Peso (benefit in purchasing inputs) and a still strong Euro, seen as a store of value. From this, we have an EBITDA for FY 2025 of between $447 million and $491 million.

Again, with EV of $4.31 billion, we would have an EV/EBITDA between 9.64x to 8.77x.

In a scenario of slight margin recovery, we could infer that Alsea would be trading very close to the industry average for EV/EBITDA (9.42x).

Let's do the same with EBIT. As with EBITDA, I will assume slightly higher EBIT margins year over year. Over the last twelve months, Alsea had a negative EBIT margin of -0.94%, well below its 5-year average of 6.09%. Something close to 5% seems credible in an optimistic scenario to me based on the assumptions we made earlier.

In this case, the projected EBIT for FY 2025 would be $223.5 million - an EV/EBIT of 19.28x. That's 34.8% higher than the industry median of 14.3x. And well, I'm being optimistic about the margin here.

Since my last post here, the stock has risen ~7.14% and is now trading at $2.25. Unless we see a sequential recovery in Europe, I don’t see much upside ahead.

However, Alsea should continue to deliver a single-digit return year-over-year through buybacks and dividends. For FY 2025, for example, we will see a dividend yield of ~3.2% and a buyback rate of 1% of shares outstanding.

Given the possibilities for accelerating buybacks, shareholder returns should not be much above 5%. Since the stock is trading very close to the $2 range, I consider the downside to be limited as well.

For me, Alsea will remain a 'Hold' with a PT close to $2.

Before revaluing based on historical margins (which will most likely give us a higher PT and a potential window to 'Buy') we will need to see the return of growth in the European segment.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: MINT: The Risks Of Bonds, Spreads, And Volatility
data.description:
The escape from volatility! Over the past month, the market has reintroduced a considerable amount of volatility, shaking investors to the core. Going beyond the equity market's rocky start to Q2, other asset classes have seen similar volatility on the heels of tariffs and other economic uncertainties. Over the past several weeks, I've authored several articles about how to capitalize on volatility in real estate and business development companies.

But the volatility also reintroduces another discussion…stability. For investors, this disrupts a period of relative peace and reemphasizes the need for downside protection. Today, we will discuss an opportunity to escape volatility while trying to add value. We will revisit a unique opportunity from PIMCO, where the fixed income manager is applying some of their special sauce to add value to short-term bonds.

Let's dive in.

Fund Overview

The PIMCO Enhanced Short Maturity Active Exchange-Traded Fund (NYSEARCA:MINT) is a short-term bond fund sponsored by PIMCO, a legendary fixed income manager. PIMCO is one of my most covered managers, mostly because they are my fixed income manager of choice. Across their lineups of exchange-traded funds and closed end funds are top performers like the PIMCO Active Bond Exchange-Traded Fund ETF (BOND) and the Corporate and Income Opportunity Fund (PTY), both of which I cover consistently.

Like many PIMCO funds, MINT invests in credit from issuers around the world, focusing primarily on the United States. The fund invests widely across bonds with short maturities from issuers of varying credit ratings. MINT avoids high-yield bonds but touches more sophisticated investments than just treasuries and bonds. For example, the top two holdings are Freddie Mac and Fannie Mae collateralized mortgage obligations.

MINT mostly invests in debt maturing inside three years, seeking to benchmark performance against the FTSE 3-Month Treasury Bill Index. The short average maturity mitigates interest rate risk and reduces volatility, a key goal of MINT.

MINT is often compared to short bond funds like the iShares 0-3 Month Treasury Bond ETF (SGOV) or the Vanguard Federal Money Market Fund (VMFXX). This makes sense, given there are large overlaps in the goals of the of the fund. That said, there are important differences that we should note.

The largest difference compared to a traditional money market mutual fund is MINT's floating share price. Traditional money market funds like VMFXX guarantee principal value at $1.00 per share. Meanwhile, MINT's share price changes as with any other ETF. The volatility comes from a variety of reasons, including interest rate risk and credit spreads, which I will outline later. In the meantime, just know that MINT is adding modest volatility to achieve a juicer yield than its money market counterparts.

All bond funds, including MINT, show monthly volatility through their distribution. This has become more noticeable as interest rates increased and the distributions from these funds became larger. MINT, like SGOV, shows steep drops at the ex-dividend date, followed by a NAV recovery through the month as interest accumulates. This creates a similar effect to the daily interest accrual of money market funds.

However, I immediately noticed that MINT is even more volatile than funds like SGOV, even considering these monthly distributions.

Most of the portfolio is allocated to investment grade credit, consistent with many short-term bond funds. However, the fund is slightly more exotic than a traditional money market or short-term bond fund, pursuing securitized credit and some interesting top ten holdings like debt from Deutsche Bank (DB) and Crown Castle International (CCI). Another example would be the Trillium Credit Card Trust at #10 of the top holdings.

For the services provided to MINT, PIMCO charges an expense ratio of 35 basis points, which is broadly consistent with the typical expense ratio of an actively managed BOND fund. However, the expenses are higher than from a treasury fund like SGOV which charges just 9 basis points, creating a higher hurdle rate for MINT to outperform.

Like many funds that offer the potential for added return through an enhancement strategy, we must ask ourselves an important question. Is the juice worth the squeeze?

Let's discuss some key contributors to MINT's volatility.

Bond Commentary

Widening credit spreads and interest rate movements are major contributors to volatility and can have significant, multi-layered impacts on bond funds, including MINT. This comes largely because of the nature of the fund's investment strategy, which aims to provide a higher yield than money market funds while maintaining stability. PIMCO achieves this by taking additional credit risk and interest rate risk selectively. For a fund like MINT, PIMCO needs to add excess return without missing.

To understand these impacts, let's talk about each separately.

Widening Credit Spreads

Credit spreads are the difference in yield between bonds of varying credit quality and their respective treasuries. Corporate bonds will broadly follow the treasury yield curve with credit spreads determining the spread over treasuries. Higher yields are demanded for bonds that carry greater perceived risk of default.

The widening of credit spreads is a signal from the market that investors perceive increased credit risk in the corporate bond market. This widening can come slowly or take place quickly, as occurred at the beginning of April. High yield bonds offer an extreme display of credit spread volatility as illustrated below.

Movements in credit spreads are driven by a variety of factors, including fears of economic downturns, declining corporate earnings, or broader market stress. In the scheme of the beginning of 2025, credit spreads quickly widened on account of tariffs and trade fears which investors worry could cause economic deterioration.

In this context, when credit spreads widen, the market is essentially requiring more compensation for taking on the same risk in corporate debt. For a fund like MINT, holding a significant portion of its assets in short-duration corporate bonds, this widening spread can have a range of negative effects and add volatility compared to a fund like SGOV that invests exclusively in treasuries.

What Happens To MINT's Corporates?

MINT's portfolio includes a mix of investment-grade corporate bonds and other income producing assets. These bonds, while considered less risky than high-yield bonds, are still subject to some credit risk or the possibility that an issuer could default. As credit spreads widen, the yields on corporate bonds must increase to remain competitive relative to risk-free treasuries.

In practice, this means that the price of these corporate bonds may fall to adjust to the higher yields now demanded by the market. The widening of credit spreads reflects increased perceptions of risk in the corporate bond market. Consequently, a fund like MINT may experience more volatility when credit spreads change due to the fund's willingness to take selective risks.

The impacts of changes in credit spread are visible for MINT, especially when compared against SGOV and funds like a short-maturity high-yield fund. MINT is more volatile than treasuries, and less volatile than a high-yield portfolio, as it should be given the credit risk taken by MINT. However, investors also have an opportunity to take advantage of minimal upside when credit spreads widen, and MINT offers a modest opportunity for capital appreciation.

Credit Migration

In addition to price declines, widening credit spreads can signal increased credit risk across the corporate bond market in general. Over a longer time frame, the deterioration of fundamentals can lead to credit rating downgrades or downward migration.

Credit migration risk is the risk that a borrower may have their rating downgraded by a rating agency. When a downgrade occurs, the yield premium will rise and the market price of the bond will fall. With the lower bond price, the holder would lose money if they sell the asset before maturity.

When a company's creditworthiness is called into question, rating agencies may lower their credit rating, which increases the cost of borrowing and typically results in further price declines for its outstanding bonds. MINT, which holds corporate bonds, might be impacted by such downgrades, especially if the fund holds bonds from companies in sensitive sectors. With an enhanced fund like MINT, this risk begins to materialize. A treasury fund, by definition, has no credit risk. MINT's credit risk is partially mitigated by the short maturities which insulate price movements for corporate bonds since the likelihood of issues being able to refinance upcoming debt is usually high relative to long-term risks.

Duration & Interest Rate Risk

The short duration piece of MINT's strategy is a critical piece of mitigating a series of risks. First, short maturities mitigate a degree of credit risk as explained above. These short-term securities also help mitigate duration risk for the fund. MINT targets a short term portfolio. In fact, nearly the entire fund matures within the next year.

This short duration is beneficial in several ways. But first, what is duration?

In fixed income, duration is a measure of how sensitive a bond's price is to changes in interest rates. It tells investors how much a bond's price will change for every 1% change in interest rates, all other factors held in isolation.

As a result, short-duration bonds are generally less sensitive to interest rate movements. At the fund level, MINT's duration is just 0.16 years, low even amongst short duration funds. This helps protect MINT from net asset value declines due to interest rate hikes, a common risk for longer duration bond funds, like BOND.

Beyond interest rate risk, MINT's short-term portfolio ensures that the fund is not as exposed to the long-term credit risk that can accumulate in bonds with longer maturities. During times of increasing credit spreads, the impact on shorter-term bonds is less severe than on bonds with longer maturities, since investors are pricing in the risk of time.

Is The Juice Worth The Squeeze?

MINT is a short duration bond fund that promises investors an additional return at the expense of minimal added volatility. The fund charges more than a typical short duration fund like SGOV, but delivers a higher initial yield. The question for investors becomes, is the added yield of MINT worth the additional risk taken by the fund. Given the recent volatility, investors can see that the risks are real and MINT is materially more volatile than a fund like SGOV or a dollar-in, dollar-out money market fund.

From my perspective, the best way to decide is to zoom all the way out and consider if MINT is delivering long-term value to shareholders. This value typically comes in total return against comparable opportunities.

Forgoing a five-year time horizon due to the perils of the pandemic, we see that MINT has outperformed competing opportunities over the past ten years. While more volatile, MINT has delivered several hundred basis points in excess return, net of fees against these more simple alternatives. Once again, PIMCO has delivered on their value proposition, earning MINT a Buy.

data.title: ETV: The Premium That Wasn't (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Thesis

The Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Buy-Write Opportunities Fund (NYSE:ETV) is a buy write CEF we have covered extensively in the past. The name represents a golden standard in the buy-write arena, and has been a reliable way throughout the years in terms of transforming equity market returns into dividends. The fund is a wrapper for equity risk, but exposes CEF-specific issues, which can account for quantifiable risk/return profiles.

In today's article, we are going to revisit ETV in light of the current macro elevated volatility, and outline why we no longer find the CEF attractive to enter from both a structural and macro perspective.

Analytics

  • AUM: $1.5 bil
  • St Dev: 12.2% (3Y)
  • Sharpe: 0.41
  • Dividend: 9.1%
  • Leverage: 0%
  • Discount: -4.5%
  • Z-stat: 0.76
  • Type: Equity Buy-Write SP 500

Volatility is back en-vogue

This year has seen the resurgence of volatility, as measured by the VIX index:

After being stuck in the teens for a long period of time, volatility is back above 20, reminiscent of what happened in 2022. The bear market of 2022 was characterized by a high level in VIX and volatility in general. While we are only 30% through 2025, it looks like tariff uncertainty will keep volatility bid this year.

A high level of volatility is usually a good thing for a fund like ETV because the options sold by the name have a higher valuation. High volatility means a high option premium. However, unlike 2022, we are not seeing an equivalent valuation bump for ETV.

The premium that wasn't

Although 2022 was a difficult year for equities, ETV got a valuation bump from volatility:

Please note the total return profile for ETV versus the SPY. As volatility increased during the year, ETV moved much higher starting in July, outperforming a simple SPY exposure. Let us look at the factors behind that move:

We can see from the above graph how the CEF suddenly moved to a premium as 2022 progressed and volatility moved higher. That premium moved back to flat to NAV in October 2022 when the equity market experienced a very significant risk-off event. Ever since early 2023 the CEF has been trading at a discount to NAV.

If we look at the 2025 performance so far, we can see that we have not yet seen a bump in the premium to NAV, despite the move higher in volatility. While there can still be a move higher here, the market is more concerned with the overall equities direction rather than the higher volatility level. We are therefore very skeptical we will trade back at a premium, mirroring the 2022 move.

Macro picture is very murky

From a historic standpoint, although we have seen a correction in prices, the S&P 500 is not cheap:

The historic average P/E ratio is roughly 16x, while the index is still trading close to 20x. And kindly keep in mind that forward earnings have not yet been adjusted to account for the tariff wars impacts. To a large extent, the current P/E ratio is adjusted by the price of the index only, not the earnings potential, which could come down significantly.

As we saw in 2022, valuations can come down quickly and violently, with a visible mean reversion. From the above graph, we can see the forward P/E moving to touch 16x in 2022, on the back of the significant market correction which saw the index down almost -24% at a certain point.

In fact, if we look at historic P/E ratios and try to adjust for the forward EPS, we get a 'valuation matrix' that offers multiple scenarios as pe Goldman:

Depending on where a user sees EPS figures and the forward P/E, the matrix above presents a permutation of results. Most scenarios do see lower equity prices, however, highlighting potential risks

ETV is a long equities fund ultimately

As a buy-write CEF, ETV is ultimately a long position in the S&P 500 via its composition:

We can see the name closely mirroring most of the index sectors, although it is currently overweight Information Technology, while underweight Financials and Health Care.

The fund will be up if equities rally, and sell-off if the overall market moves lower. As we have seen from the discount section above, despite expectations, the CEF has not moved to a premium this year.

Make no mistake about it - as an investor in ETV you are long the S&P 500, and via this CEF structure you are purely extracting dividends from the index return. Do not think of ETV as a fixed income fund because it is not. The vehicle simply uses options overlays in order to cleanly pump out dividends. The fund does not use the premiums to help a NAV accretion.

The fund's current option overlay does leave room for upside, but it conservatively covers 95% of the portfolio:

The average days to expiration for the options is 15 days, and the '% out of the money' is 4.1%.

Conclusion

ETV is an equity buy-write CEF that has a very robust historic performance. The name experienced a high premium to NAV in 2022 when we had a bear market but a high volatility figure, thus making options writing more profitable. Despite our expectations for a similar structural shift this year, the discount to NAV has not moved to a premium, despite the higher structural VIX. Given macro equity concerns, we are downgrading the fund to 'Hold'.
